text:
By no later than [time] notify the solicitors for the applicant, [applicant firm name], of the arrangements that will be made for the return of the person to be protected to the jurisdiction of England and Wales;

Return or cause the return of the person to be protected [name] to the jurisdiction of England and Wales in accordance with the arrangements notified at sub-paragraph b. (above) and by no later than [time and date].

Upon the return of the person to be protected [name] to the jurisdiction of England and Wales the respondent, or each of them, shall be prohibited from removing the said person to be protected from the jurisdiction of England and Wales, and from instructing or encouraging any other person to so remove them.

(In the event that the applicant is not the person to be protected or a Local Authority) The applicant [applicant name] shall have leave to commence proceedings for the protection of the person to be protected [name] pursuant to the Forced Marriage (Civil Protection) Act 2007

(If applicable) [Name of litigation friend] shall be appointed as the litigation friend of the person to be protected [name].

The matter shall be adjourned and relisted to allow the respondent to make representations about this order (or any part of it) at a hearing before a [High Court Judge of the Family Division] / [Judge of the Family Court] sitting in private at [place] on [date] at [time], with a time estimate of [time estimate].

The respondent shall attend the hearing listed pursuant to paragraph [para number] (above) together with solicitors and/or counsel if so instructed. A penal notice is attached to this paragraph.

The respondent may, if so advised, file and serve a statement of evidence by no later than [time and date].

Costs reserved.

Dated [date]

Note to Arresting Officer:

Under section 63CA of the Family Law Act 1996, breach of a forced marriage protection order is a criminal offence punishable by up to five years’ imprisonment. It is an arrestable offence and it is not necessary to obtain a warrant.

“A person who without reasonable excuse does anything that the person is prohibited from doing by a forced marriage protection order is guilty of an offence.”

Family Law Act 1996, section 63CA (1)
